claims.reviewNote: The official text says the Articles were agreed by the archbishops, bishops, and clergy in the London Convocation held in 1562; the collective is represented as the issuing body, not as an individual author.

The denominational overview says the creed was a compilation rather than a work of the apostles and was adopted in its present form before the end of the fourth century; no individual creator or numeric interval is asserted.

The official LCMS overview says the creed is anonymous and probably originated in the middle of the sixth century (while its table gives a broader sixth-to-eighth-century range); the catalog conservatively exposes an approximate sixth-century label and no machine-filterable year or creator.
The LCMS history states directly that Philip Melanchthon composed the Augsburg Confession in preparation for the Diet of Augsburg in 1530.
USCCB says the catechism originated when the bishops at the Third Plenary Council of Baltimore decided in 1884 to publish a national catechism; the council is represented as issuing body.
The CRC introduction identifies Guido de Brès as chief author and says he prepared the confession in 1561.
The CRC introduction says the statements were adopted by the Synod of Dort, which met in 1618-1619; the synod is represented as issuing body.
The Holy See catechism identifies the cited confession as that of the fourth ecumenical council at Chalcedon in 451; the council is represented as issuing body.

The GOARCH account pairs the Council of Jerusalem in 1672 with the Confession of Faith by Patriarch Dositheos of Jerusalem in 1672; Dositheos is represented as author rather than relabeling the council.
The Holy See commission dates the Council of Trent to 1545-1563 and identifies the teaching as conciliar; the council is represented as issuing body.

The CRC introduction dates the catechism to 1563 and describes the project as the work of a team of ministers and university theologians; the team is represented as drafting body without inferring sole authorship.

Founders says Particular Baptist churches drafted the confession in 1677 and put it forward at a General Assembly in 1689. Only 1677 is materialized as composition; 1689 remains work-title and reception context, not a continuous date interval.

The Holy See commission says Nicaea produced a creed in 325 and the First Council of Constantinople completed the present Niceno-Constantinopolitan text in 381. The catalog therefore represents composition as 381 only and Constantinople as the revising body; 325 remains origin context, not a composition-date match.
The institutional archive says Philaret wrote the catechism and that its initial publication was in 1823; the stronger source wording supports author rather than compiler.
The OPC states the Westminster Assembly produced the Confession of Faith in 1646; the collective is represented as drafting body.
The OPC states the Westminster Assembly produced the Larger Catechism in 1647; the collective is represented as drafting body.
The OPC states the Westminster Assembly produced the Shorter Catechism in 1647; the collective is represented as drafting body.
